topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon stretched before me, an endless, humming expanse of beige walls and the faint scent of old paper. I kept tapping my foot against the worn linoleum, a quick, uneven rhythm that seemed to vibrate up through the soles of my shoes. Every minute felt like an eternity, a slow, heavy drag. I traced the faded outline of a constellation on my bedroom ceiling, my gaze snagging on the space where memories used to cluster—the shared laughter, the whispered secrets. A tightness coiled in my chest, a knot that refused to loosen, demanding release. I picked up my phone, scrolling through old photos, searching for a flicker of recognition, a ghost of a shared glance. Nothing. Just faces, smiling, oblivious. Then I looked at the framed picture of Liam and me, standing awkwardly by a poorly painted mural. I traced his jawline with a fingertip, a strange hollowness blooming in my stomach. Why did this feeling cling so fiercely?

[story 2]

I paced the length of the apartment three times before stopping abruptly in front of the window. The city lights below seemed too sharp, too defined, mocking the dull ache inside me. My hands kept flexing, the skin feeling strangely clammy. I replayed the last conversation, the easy flow of our usual banter, trying to pinpoint the moment the shift occurred. It wasn't a dramatic falling out; it was a gradual fading, like a radio signal losing strength. I felt a desperate need to reach out, to pull him back into the shared orbit we once occupied, but my voice felt thick and inadequate. I tried calling, then texting, the screen illuminating my face with a strained intensity. Silence. The quiet in the room amplified the frantic drumming in my ears. I turned away from the window, the need to move, to *do* something concrete, overwhelming my ability to simply sit still.

[story 3]

The silence in the hallway was oppressive. I walked back and forth, a caged animal pacing the familiar stretch of floorboards. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my temples. Each breath felt shallow, insufficient. I kept catching my reflection in the darkened glass of the elevator doors, searching for some sign of the energy that usually animated me—the spark that made the world feel navigable. It wasn't there. Just a hollow echo. I started rearranging objects on my desk, stacking books with unnecessary precision, a futile attempt to impose order on the internal chaos. I needed a reaction, some acknowledgment that this disconnect wasn't just happening inside my head. I wanted him to look at me, to see the years we’d built, to feel the void where my presence used to be. The urge to smash something, anything, surfaced briefly, quickly suppressed by a wave of sheer, bewildered exhaustion.
